title: Error Handling — Application Stack Traces Exposing Internals

value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Add-on for Java logging.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=app` log4j/json sourcetypes.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Route samples to restricted index; (2) work with dev teams to scrub patterns; (3) alert on increases post-release; (4) validate no secrets in messages via regex vault patterns.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=app earliest=-24h log_level IN ("ERROR","FATAL")
| regex _raw="(?i)(stack trace|SQLException|traceback|ORA-\d{5})"
| stats count by sourcetype, service, host
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Error Handling — Application Stack Traces Exposing Internals (SI-11)** — Finds logs containing stack traces or SQL fragments that may violate SI-11 error handling by revealing sensitive implementation details.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=app` log4j/json sourcetypes. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): HTTP Event Collector (HEC), Splunk Add-on for Java logging. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: app.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=app, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters rows matching a pattern with `regex`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by sourcetype, service, host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (noisy services), Time chart (error spikes), Single value (matches)
grandmaExplanation: We watch for finds logs containing stack traces or sql fragments that may violate error handling by revealing sensitive implementation details. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
